title: How to Create a Financial Practice That Includes Your Whole Life
body:
Your Financial Life Isn't Broken. Your Relationship With It Is.

Here's the thing. You've tried the budgets. You've downloaded the apps. You've probably even read the "get rich quick" books or the ones promising financial freedom in 30 days. And yet, here you are. Still feeling that gnawing anxiety. Still wondering why money feels like a constant uphill battle, a source of stress rather than security. You're not alone. So many of us are trapped in this cycle, believing that if we just find the right spreadsheet, the magic formula, everything will click. But it doesn't, does it? That's because the problem isn't your spreadsheet. It's not your bank balance. It's deeper. Much, much deeper.

Truth is, your financial life isn't separate from your emotional life, your spiritual life, your relationships, or your well-being. It's all intertwined. When you try to treat money as a purely logical, mathematical problem, you're ignoring 90% of what's actually going on. You're ignoring the fear, the shame, the ancestral patterns, the limiting beliefs that are running the show behind the scenes. And until you address those, until you integrate your whole self into your financial practice, you'll always feel like you're swimming against the current. The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It truly does. It's a wound that impacts everything.

Beyond the Numbers: Money as a Spiritual Practice

You might be thinking, "Spiritual practice? Kalesh, I just need to pay my bills." I get it. I really do. For so long, we've been taught to compartmentalize. Money is for the head, spirituality is for the heart. Never the twain shall meet. But what if that separation is precisely why we're struggling? What if money, when approached with intention and awareness, can become a powerful tool for self-discovery, for healing, for living a more authentic and joyful life? Lynne Twist, in her important book The Soul of Money (paid link), talks about sufficiency - the idea that there is enough. Not just enough money, but enough love, enough time, enough joy. This isn't some fluffy, wishful thinking. It's a radical reframing of our relationship with resources, moving us from a mindset of scarcity to one of abundance. It's about recognizing that our financial choices are reflections of our deepest values, our fears, our hopes.

Think about it. When you make a spending decision, what's really driving it? Is it a genuine need? A desire for connection? A craving for comfort? Or is it an attempt to numb an uncomfortable feeling? To prove your worth? To keep up with someone else? These aren't financial decisions in a vacuum. They're deeply personal, emotionally charged acts. And pretending they're not is like trying to drive a car with one foot on the gas and the other on the brake. You're just creating internal friction, burning yourself out, and not getting anywhere fast.

Integrating your whole life into your financial practice means bringing consciousness to these unconscious drivers. It means asking yourself the hard questions. It means being willing to look at the parts of yourself you'd rather ignore. It means understanding that your money story isn't just about what's in your bank account; it's about the stories you tell yourself about money, about your worth, about what's possible for you. Brad Klontz, a pioneer in financial psychology, calls these "money scripts." These are often unconscious beliefs about money formed in childhood that dictate our financial behaviors as adults. They can be incredibly powerful, even when they're illogical or detrimental. You feel that? That little tug when you think about your spending habits, your savings, your debt? That's your money script talking.

Confronting Your Money Wounds: Shame, Fear, and Worth

Let's be honest. For most of us, talking about money brings up a lot of uncomfortable feelings. Shame. Fear. Guilt. Inadequacy. We're taught from a young age that money is a private matter, often a taboo subject. This secrecy breeds shame. We compare ourselves to others, often making assumptions about their financial situations that are completely untrue. We carry the weight of past financial mistakes, allowing them to define our present and future. Does that land? This isn't just about making more money or budgeting better. It's about healing the emotional baggage you carry around money.

Consider debt, for example. For many, debt isn't just a number. It's a heavy cloak of shame, a constant reminder of perceived failures or shortcomings. Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Do you tense up? Does your stomach clench? Do you feel a flush of heat? That's your body's way of telling you this isn't just a logical problem. It's an emotional one. And you can't solve an emotional problem with a purely logical solution. You can't shame yourself out of debt. You can't fear your way to financial freedom. You need a different approach.

This is where practices like radical acceptance come in. Tara Brach's book Radical Acceptance (paid link) isn't about money, but its principles are deeply applicable. It's about acknowledging what is, without judgment. It's about looking at your financial reality - your debt, your income, your spending habits - with a compassionate, curious gaze, rather than a critical, shaming one. It's about saying, "Okay, this is where I am right now. This is my reality. And it's okay to feel whatever I'm feeling about it." This isn't about resignation; it's about creating a foundation of self-compassion from which real change can emerge.

Barbara Huson, in her work on "overearning" and "sacred success," highlights how often women, in particular, under-earn due to deeply ingrained beliefs about their worth and societal expectations. It's not about lacking skills or opportunities; it's about an internal block that prevents them from claiming their full financial power. This isn't just about making more money; it's about recognizing your inherent value and aligning your financial reality with that truth. It's about understanding that your worth isn't tied to your net worth. But your belief in your worth absolutely impacts your net worth.

The Practice of Conscious Money Flow

So, how do you actually do this? How do you create a financial practice that includes your whole life? It starts with awareness. It starts with slowing down. It starts with seeing money as energy, as a flow, rather than a static thing to be hoarded or feared. Think of it like this: your relationship with money is a reflection of your relationship with yourself. If you're constantly critical, judgmental, or avoidant with yourself, you'll likely be the same with your money.

One of the most powerful practices is simply tracking. Not just tracking where your money goes, but how you feel when it goes there. How do you feel when you pay a bill? When you make a purchase? When you receive money? This isn't about judgment; it's about observation. It's about becoming a financial anthropologist of your own life. You'll start to notice patterns. You'll see where you spend out of alignment with your values, where you spend to cope, where you spend to impress. And you'll also see where you spend joyfully, consciously, and in alignment with who you truly are. The Art of Money (paid link) by Bari Tessler is an excellent resource for this kind of conscious money tracking and deep dive into your financial inner world.

Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. Whether it's your credit card balance, your student loan debt, or the amount in your savings account. Just sit with it. Don't try to fix it. Don't try to analyze it. Just feel what comes up. This willingness to be present with discomfort is a cornerstone of any truly integrated financial practice. Sam Harris, the neuroscientist and philosopher, often speaks about the importance of being present with our subjective experience, even the unpleasant parts. This isn't about wallowing; it's about gathering information. It's about understanding the terrain before you try to work through it.

Designing Your Intentional Financial Life

Once you've started building awareness, the next step is intention. What do you actually want your financial life to look like? What values do you want it to reflect? This isn't just about setting arbitrary savings goals or debt repayment targets. It's about connecting those numbers to your deepest desires for your life. Do you want more freedom? More time with loved ones? To contribute to causes you care about? To create something beautiful in the world? How does money support that vision?

Your financial practice isn't just about managing what you have; it's about creating what you want. It's about consciously designing a life that feels rich, not just in dollars, but in meaning, connection, and joy. This means making choices that align with your values, even if they're unconventional. It might mean saying no to certain expenses that don't truly serve you. It might mean saying yes to investments in experiences or education that feed your soul, even if they don't have an immediate financial return. It's about understanding that every dollar you spend or save is a vote for the kind of life you want to live. Right?!

This process is fluid, not rigid. It's not about perfection; it's about progress. There will be days when you feel completely in alignment, and days when you feel like you've fallen off the wagon. That's okay. The practice is in returning, again and again, to awareness, to compassion, to intention. It's in extending yourself the same grace you'd offer a dear friend struggling with their finances.

The Power of Generosity and Reciprocity

A truly integrated financial life also embraces generosity. This isn't about giving away what you can't afford. It's about understanding that money, like all energy, flows. When you give, you create space to receive. This isn't some cosmic accounting trick; it's a shift in mindset. When you operate from a place of sufficiency, you recognize that there's enough to share. Generosity doesn't just benefit the recipient; it shifts your own internal world from scarcity to abundance. It reminds you that you are capable of giving, that you have something to offer, that you are part of a larger interconnected web.

This also extends to how you receive. Are you open to receiving compliments, support, opportunities, and yes, money? Many of us have blocks around receiving, believing we're not worthy, or that it's selfish. But true reciprocity requires both giving and receiving. If you only give, you'll eventually burn out. If you only receive, you'll feel disconnected. A healthy financial flow involves both. It's a dance, a dynamic exchange. This isn't just about charity; it's about conscious consumption, supporting businesses and individuals whose values align with yours, and investing in your community. It's about understanding that your financial choices have ripple effects far beyond your own bank account.

This isn't just a mental exercise. It's embodied. Your relationship with money shows up in your posture, your breath, your gut feelings. When you cultivate a sense of generosity and reciprocity, you literally shift your physiology. You move from a state of contraction and fear to one of expansion and possibility. This isn't just "woo-woo"; it's practical. When your body is relaxed and open, you're more creative, more resilient, and better able to make clear, aligned decisions about your money.

FAQ: Making Peace with Your Money

What if I have a lot of debt and feel overwhelmed?

It's completely normal to feel overwhelmed by debt. The first step isn't to pay it all off tomorrow; it's to acknowledge the feeling without judgment. Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Then, get clear on the numbers. Don't hide from them. Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. Once you have a clear picture, you can start making a plan. Even small, consistent actions, combined with a shift in your emotional relationship to the debt, can create significant change. Remember, the money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. Address the emotional component first.

How do I stop comparing my financial situation to others?

Comparison is a thief of joy, especially when it comes to money. Most people only show you their highlight reel, not their struggles. To combat this, practice gratitude for what you do have. Focus on your own financial journey and progress, rather than someone else's perceived success. Consciously limit exposure to social media that triggers comparison. Remind yourself that your worth is inherent, not dependent on your net worth or what others have. This takes consistent effort, but it's crucial for your peace of mind.

Is this just about making more money?

No, not at all. While increasing income can be part of a healthy financial practice, this approach is fundamentally about healing your relationship with money, regardless of your current income level. It's about finding sufficiency, meaning, and alignment. You can be wealthy and still have a toxic relationship with money, experiencing constant anxiety or dissatisfaction. Conversely, you can have a modest income and feel deeply secure and joyful in your financial life. It's about the internal world, not just the external numbers. The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet, whether you have a lot or a little.

Look, creating a financial practice that includes your whole life isn't a quick fix. It's a journey. It requires patience, self-compassion, and a willingness to look inward. But it's also the most powerful work you can do to transform your relationship with money, and by extension, with yourself and your life. It's about moving from a place of fear and scarcity to one of freedom, purpose, and genuine abundance. It's about recognizing that you are worthy of a rich, fulfilling life, and that your financial choices can be a direct expression of that truth.

Your challenge today is simple: Take 5 minutes. Find a quiet space. Close your eyes. And just breathe. Then, notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Don't judge it. Just notice. That's it. That's the first step on a new path.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Transform your relationship with money. Learn how to build a financial practice that integrates with your entire life, reducing stress and fostering security.
author: Kalesh
